section.title: The Drought and the Prophet's Laments
section.sectionRationale: A devastating drought exposes Judah's helplessness even as false prophets promise peace, and God tells Jeremiah not to pray for a people so given over to judgment that not even Moses or Samuel could turn it back. In the middle of this darkness, Jeremiah confesses that God's words were his joy and delight, and receives a promise to be made a fortified wall against his opponents.
section.thesis: When drought and sword and famine come as covenant judgment, false prophets' promises of peace prove empty, and God's refusal to hear intercession shows how far Judah's sin has gone, yet even here Jeremiah's own confession, that God's words were eaten as joy, becomes the pattern for enduring faithfully under God's discipline while trusting His promise to sustain His messenger.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the drought that exposes Judah's helplessness and its people's confession of guilt; God's refusal to hear prayer for this people and His exposure of the false prophets promising peace; a second, more desperate cycle of lament asking whether God has utterly rejected Judah; God's severe reply that not even Moses or Samuel could turn back this judgment, rooted in the sins of Manasseh; and Jeremiah's own confession that God's words were his joy, met with God's promise to make him an unbreakable wall.

verses.text: This is the word of the LORD that came to Jeremiah concerning the {{batstsoreth|drought}}:
“Judah mourns and her gates languish. Her people wail for the land, and a cry goes up from Jerusalem.

The nobles send their servants for water; they go to the cisterns, but find no water; 
their jars return empty. They are ashamed and humiliated; they cover their heads.
The ground is cracked because no rain has fallen on the land. The farmers are ashamed; they cover their heads.
Even the doe in the field deserts her newborn fawn because there is no grass.

Wild donkeys stand on barren heights; they pant for air like jackals; 
their eyes fail for lack of pasture.”
Although our iniquities testify against us, O LORD, act for the sake of Your name. Indeed, our rebellions are many; we have sinned against You.
O {{tiqvah|Hope}} of Israel, its Savior in times of distress, why are You like a stranger in the land, like a traveler who stays but a night?

Why are You like a man taken by surprise, like a warrior powerless to save? Yet You are among us, O LORD, and we are called by Your name. 
Do not forsake us!

units.label: Do not pray for this people

verses.text:
This is what the LORD says about this people: 
“Truly they love to wander; they have not restrained their feet. So the LORD does not accept them; He will now remember their guilt 
and call their sins to account.”
Then the LORD said to me, “Do not pray for the well-being of this people.

Although they may fast, I will not listen to their cry; although they may offer burnt offerings and grain offerings, I will not accept them. Instead, I will finish them off by sword and famine and plague.”
“Ah, Lord GOD!” I replied, “Look, the prophets are telling them, ‘You will not see the sword or suffer famine, but I will give you lasting peace in this place.’”

“The prophets are prophesying lies in My name,” replied the LORD. “I did not send them or appoint them or speak to them. They are prophesying to you a false vision, a worthless divination, the futility and delusion of their own minds.

Therefore this is what the LORD says about the prophets who prophesy in My name: I did not send them, yet they say, ‘No sword or famine will touch this land.’ 
By sword and famine these very prophets will meet their end!

And the people to whom they prophesy will be thrown into the streets of Jerusalem because of famine and sword. There will be no one to bury them or their wives, their sons or their daughters. I will pour out their own evil upon them.

You are to speak this word to them: 
‘My eyes overflow with tears; day and night they do not cease, for the virgin daughter of my people has been shattered by a crushing blow, 
a severely grievous wound.
If I go out to the country, I see those slain by the sword; if I enter the city, I see those ravaged by famine! For both prophet and priest travel to a land they do not know.’”

Have You rejected Judah completely? Do You despise Zion? Why have You stricken us so that we are beyond healing? We hoped for peace, but no good has come, and for the time of healing, but there was only terror.

We acknowledge our wickedness, O LORD, the guilt of our fathers; 
indeed, we have sinned against You.
For the sake of Your name do not despise us; do not disgrace Your glorious throne. Remember Your covenant with us; do not break it.

Can the worthless idols of the nations bring rain? Do the skies alone send showers? Is this not by You, O LORD our God? So we put our hope in You, 
for You have done all these things.

units.label: Even Moses and Samuel
units.note: judgment rooted in the sins of Manasseh

verses.text: Then the LORD said to me: “Even if Moses and Samuel should stand before Me, My heart would not go out to this people. Send them from My presence, and let them go.

If they ask you, ‘Where shall we go?’ you are to tell them that this is what the LORD says: 
‘Those destined for death, to death; those destined for the sword, to the sword; those destined for famine, to famine; and those destined for captivity, to captivity.’
I will appoint over them four kinds of destroyers, declares the LORD: the sword to kill, the dogs to drag away, and the birds of the air and beasts of the earth to devour and destroy.
I will make them a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth because of what Manasseh son of Hezekiah king of Judah did in Jerusalem.
Who will have pity on you, O Jerusalem? Who will mourn for you? Who will turn aside to ask about your welfare?

You have forsaken Me, declares the LORD. You have turned your back. So I will stretch out My hand against you and I will destroy you; 
I am weary of showing compassion.
I will scatter them with a winnowing fork at the gates of the land. I will bereave and destroy My people who have not turned from their ways.
I will make their widows more numerous than the sand of the sea. I will bring a destroyer at noon against the mothers of young men. I will suddenly bring upon them anguish and dismay.

The mother of seven will grow faint; she will breathe her last breath. Her sun will set while it is still day; she will be disgraced and humiliated. And the rest I will put to the sword in the presence of their enemies,” declares the LORD.

units.label: Your words were found, and I ate them

verses.text: Woe to me, my mother, that you have borne me, a man of strife and conflict in all the land. I have neither lent nor borrowed, yet everyone curses me.

The LORD said: 
“Surely I will deliver you for a good purpose; surely I will intercede with your enemy in your time of trouble, in your time of distress.
Can anyone smash iron, iron from the north, or bronze?
Your wealth and your treasures I will give up as plunder, without charge for all your sins within all your borders.
Then I will enslave you to your enemies in a land you do not know, for My anger will kindle a fire that will burn against you.”

You understand, O LORD; remember me and attend to me. Avenge me against my persecutors. In Your patience, do not take me away. 
Know that I endure reproach for Your honor.

Your words were found, and I {{akal|ate}} them. Your words became my joy 
and my heart’s delight. For I bear Your name, O LORD God of Hosts.
I never sat with the band of revelers, nor did I celebrate with them. Because Your hand was on me, I sat alone, for You have filled me with indignation.

Why is my pain unending, and my wound incurable, 
refusing to be healed? You have indeed become like a mirage to me, water that is not there.

Therefore this is what the LORD says: 
“If you return, I will restore you; you will stand in My presence. And if you speak words that are noble instead of worthless, you will be My spokesman. It is they who must turn to you, but you must not turn to them.
Then I will make you a {{chomah|wall}} to this people, a fortified wall of bronze; they will fight against you but will not overcome you, for I am with you to save and deliver you, declares the LORD.
I will deliver you from the hand of the wicked and redeem you from the grasp of the ruthless.”

batstsoreth.gloss: Drought, a withholding of rain. Deuteronomy 28 names drought among the covenant curses for disobedience, so this natural disaster carries theological weight, a visible sign that the covenant relationship has been broken.

tiqvah.gloss: Hope, expectation. Jeremiah addresses God directly as the Hope of Israel even while lamenting His apparent silence, modeling faith that keeps calling on God by His true character even in the middle of unanswered longing.

akal.gloss: To eat, consume, or devour. Jeremiah describes finding and eating God's words as joy and delight, an image of Scripture becoming genuine nourishment, later echoed when Ezekiel is told to eat a scroll, Ezekiel 3:1-3.

chomah.gloss: A wall, especially a city's defensive wall. God promises to make Jeremiah a fortified wall of bronze against his opponents, an image of unbreakable protection that anticipates Christ's promise that the gates of hell will not prevail against His church.

basic.context: A severe drought strikes Judah, cisterns run dry, animals are abandoned, and even the ground cracks open. The people cry out, confessing their guilt, yet God tells Jeremiah not to intercede, exposing that false prophets have been promising peace and prosperity in His name without His sending them. A second wave of lament asks whether God has utterly rejected Judah, and God's answer is severe: not even Moses or Samuel, Israel's greatest intercessors, could turn back this judgment, decreed because of what Manasseh did generations before. In the middle of this darkness, Jeremiah confesses that God's words were found and eaten as joy, and receives God's own promise to make him an unbreakable wall against his opponents.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage combines communal lament liturgy, divine refusal, and personal confession in a way that models honest faith under judgment: the people's prayer sounds pious, yet God exposes that repentance detached from a changed life cannot avert consequences already set in motion. Jeremiah's own confession in 15:10-18 is startlingly raw, bordering on accusing God of becoming like a mirage, yet God does not rebuke this honesty; He answers it with a call to return and a promise of strength.
basic.originalLanguages: The batstsoreth, drought, becomes the occasion for exposing both false prophets and Judah's own hollow repentance. Jeremiah calls God the tiqvah, Hope, of Israel even while lamenting His apparent absence. Later Jeremiah confesses that he akal, ate, God's words as joy and delight, an image of internalizing revelation as nourishment. And God promises to make him a chomah, wall, of bronze that cannot be overcome.
basic.typology: Jeremiah eating God's words as joy and delight anticipates the pattern later commanded of Ezekiel, who is told to eat a scroll, Ezekiel 3:1-3, and ultimately points to Christ, the living Word, whom believers are nourished by, John 6:35. God's promise to make Jeremiah an unbreakable wall anticipates Christ's promise that the gates of hell will not prevail against His church, Matthew 16:18.
basic.christ: Jeremiah's confession, Your words were found, and I ate them, and they became my joy, anticipates Jesus' own words, man shall not live on bread alone but on every word that proceeds from the mouth of God, Matthew 4:4, and finds its fullest expression in Christ, the Word made flesh, who is Himself the bread of life, John 6:35. And where God tells Jeremiah not even Moses or Samuel could intercede successfully for this generation, the gospel reveals a greater intercessor still, Christ, whose intercession never fails, Hebrews 7:25.
basic.god: God is willing to hear honest lament, yet He will not accept repentance detached from a changed life, and He exposes false prophets speaking comfortable lies in His name without His sending them. His refusal to hear intercession even from figures as great as Moses and Samuel reveals the severity of accumulated, generational sin, yet His personal answer to Jeremiah, offering renewed strength and protection, shows His discipline is never separated from His care for those who remain faithful to Him.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 32:11-14 shows Moses interceding successfully for Israel, one of the two great intercessors named here. Ezekiel 3:1-3 pictures a prophet eating a scroll, echoing Jeremiah's image. Matthew 4:4 has Jesus quote the principle that man lives by every word from God's mouth. Hebrews 7:25 promises that Christ's intercession, unlike any merely human intercessor, never fails.
basic.oneStory: This is the sober reality that a people can sin themselves past the point where even the greatest intercessors could turn back the consequences, and yet within that same darkness, God nourishes His faithful servant with His own words as joy and promises him unbreakable strength, patterns fulfilled in Christ, the true Word who feeds His people and the greater Intercessor whose prayers for His own never fail.

context.literary: This unit combines two cycles of drought, communal prayer, and divine refusal, 14:1-15:9, followed by Jeremiah's own extended personal confession and God's reply, 15:10-21, forming one of the most emotionally intense sections in the whole book.
context.historical: The drought likely reflects an actual agricultural crisis during Jeremiah's ministry, interpreted prophetically as covenant judgment; the reference to Manasseh, 15:4, recalls the idolatrous king whose reign, 2 Kings 21, is blamed even generations later for sealing Judah's fate.
context.canonical: This passage's naming of Moses and Samuel as Israel's great intercessors, and its portrait of the prophet eating God's word as joy, both find deep resonance in the New Testament's teaching on Christ's intercession and Christ as the living Word.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that a nation can reach a point of accumulated, generational sin so severe that even history's greatest intercessors could not turn back its consequences, while also honoring Jeremiah's raw, personal struggle with his calling as a legitimate part of faithful ministry that God meets with correction and renewed promise rather than rejection.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical crisis, drought, false prophecy, and Jeremiah's own personal anguish, culminating in judgment tied to a specific king's sins; it is not a claim that any nation's fate is fixed regardless of later repentance. The enduring, prescriptive truth is that God distinguishes true repentance from its outward performance, and that honest struggle brought to God, even when raw, is met with His correction and renewed strength rather than abandonment.

debates.question: In 15:19, why does God tell Jeremiah, if you return, I will restore you, implying Jeremiah himself needed to repent?

views.label: Jeremiah's complaint had crossed into inappropriate accusation
views.case: Jeremiah's words in verse 18, You have become like a mirage to me, border on charging God with unfaithfulness; God's call to return addresses this specific overreach in Jeremiah's lament, correcting rather than merely comforting him.

views.label: A general call matching the pattern God requires of all His people
views.case: Others read this as God applying to His prophet the same standard of wholehearted return He requires of Judah throughout the book, showing that even faithful messengers are not exempt from the call to repentance and renewed trust.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree God treats Jeremiah's honest lament seriously enough to respond directly to it, correcting where necessary, while still renewing His commission and promise of protection over him, showing that honest struggle and continued calling are not mutually exclusive.

study.originalLanguages: The batstsoreth, drought, functions as more than weather; Deuteronomy 28 lists it among the covenant's sanctioned curses, so its arrival testifies to broken covenant loyalty. Jeremiah addresses God as the tiqvah, Hope, of Israel even in the middle of apparent divine absence. He later confesses that he akal, ate, God's words, receiving them as joy and delight rather than mere information. And God promises to make him a chomah, wall, of bronze, an image of a city's defensive fortification now applied to the prophet's own unbreakable protection.

typology.type: Jeremiah eating God's words as joy and delight, Jeremiah 15:16
typology.fulfillment: Echoed when Ezekiel is told to eat a scroll, Ezekiel 3:1-3, and ultimately fulfilled in Christ, the living Word who is Himself the bread of life, John 6:35.

typology.type: God's promise to make Jeremiah a fortified wall of bronze, Jeremiah 15:20
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ's promise that the gates of hell will not prevail against His church, Matthew 16:18.

study.christ: Jeremiah's confession, Your words were found, and I ate them, and they became my joy, anticipates Jesus' own words, man shall not live on bread alone but on every word that proceeds from the mouth of God, Matthew 4:4, and finds its fullest expression in Christ, the Word made flesh, who is Himself the bread of life, John 6:35. Where God tells Jeremiah that not even Moses or Samuel could intercede successfully for this generation, the gospel reveals a greater intercessor still, Christ, who always lives to make intercession for those who come to God through Him, an intercession that never fails, Hebrews 7:25.
study.god: God is willing to hear honest lament, yet He will not accept repentance detached from a changed life, and He exposes false prophets speaking comfortable lies in His name without His sending them. His refusal to hear intercession even from figures as great as Moses and Samuel reveals the severity of Judah's accumulated, generational sin, rooted in Manasseh's own idolatry, yet His personal answer to Jeremiah, offering renewed strength, protection, and deliverance, shows His discipline is never separated from His steadfast care for those who remain faithful to Him.

crossReferences.note: Shows Moses interceding successfully for Israel, one of the two great intercessors God names in 15:1.
Pictures a prophet eating a scroll, closely echoing Jeremiah's own image of eating God's words.
Jesus quotes the principle that man lives by every word that comes from the mouth of God.
Promises that Christ's intercession, unlike any merely human intercessor, never fails and never ends.

study.oneStory: This is the sober reality that a people can sin themselves past the point where even the greatest intercessors could turn back the consequences, and yet within that same darkness, God nourishes His faithful servant with His own words as joy and promises him unbreakable strength, patterns fulfilled in Christ, the true Word who feeds His people and the greater Intercessor whose prayers for His own never fail.

sermon.title: Christmas at the Castle, Part 3
sermon.why: Carson traces the two cycles of drought, prayer, and God's refusal across Jeremiah 14 to 15, along with Jeremiah's own prayers and God's response, exactly the movements this study covers.

quiz.q: What natural disaster forms the backdrop of Jeremiah 14?

options.text: A severe drought, leaving cisterns empty and the ground cracked.
options.note: Verses 1-6 describe this disaster in detail.

options.text: A great flood covering the land.
options.note: This is the opposite of the described drought.

options.text: A plague of locusts destroying the crops.
options.note: Locusts are not the specific disaster named in this passage.

options.text: A devastating earthquake striking Jerusalem.
options.note: Not the disaster described here; the chapter specifically concerns drought and lack of rain.

quiz.q: Why does God refuse to accept Judah's fasting and offerings in 14:12?

options.text: Because He will not accept them; He intends to finish the people off by sword, famine, and plague regardless.
options.note: Verse 12 states this directly.

options.text: Because God had already decided to bless the nation instead.
options.note: This directly contradicts the severe judgment announced in the verse.

options.text: Because the priests had stopped offering sacrifices altogether.
options.note: This contradicts the text, which describes fasting and offerings still being made.

options.text: Because the offerings were not the correct kind prescribed by the law.
options.note: The issue is not ritual correctness but a repentance detached from genuine change of life.

quiz.q: What does God say about the false prophets in 14:14-15?

options.text: He did not send them, and they will themselves perish by the very sword and famine they denied would come.
options.note: Verses 14-15 state this outcome directly.

options.text: They will be rewarded for comforting the people.
options.note: This contradicts the judgment pronounced on them in verse 15.

options.text: They were sent by God but exceeded their instructions.
options.note: God explicitly says He did not send them at all.

options.text: They spoke truthfully but were simply misunderstood.
options.note: God explicitly calls their message lies, false visions, and delusion, not truth that was misunderstood.

quiz.q: According to 15:1, why would even Moses or Samuel be unable to change the outcome?

options.text: Because God's heart would not go out to this people; He tells Jeremiah to send them away from His presence.
options.note: Verse 1 states this directly, naming Moses and Samuel specifically.

options.text: Because Judah had already been fully forgiven and needed no further intercession.
options.note: This directly contradicts the severe judgment announced in the passage.

options.text: Because Moses and Samuel had themselves sinned against God.
options.note: The text does not fault Moses or Samuel personally; it names them as history's greatest intercessors.

options.text: Because Moses and Samuel were no longer living and could not physically intervene.
options.note: The point concerns the severity of judgment, since the verse imagines them hypothetically standing before God regardless of their being alive.

quiz.q: What does Jeremiah say about God's words in 15:16?

options.text: That he found them and ate them, and they became his joy and his heart's delight.
options.note: Verse 16 states this directly.

options.text: That he never actually received a word from God at all.
options.note: This contradicts the entire premise of Jeremiah's prophetic ministry and this very verse.

options.text: That he refused to receive them because of his suffering.
options.note: This contradicts his stated response of joy and delight.

options.text: That they brought him no comfort in his distress.
options.note: This contradicts the verse's description of God's words as joy and delight.

questions: The people's prayer in 14:7-9 sounds pious, yet God does not accept it as genuine repentance. What is the difference between saying the right words to God and actually turning back to Him?
The false prophets promised peace that was not coming. Where might you be tempted to believe comforting words over the harder truth God is actually saying to you?
God says not even Moses or Samuel could turn back this judgment. Have you ever faced consequences that prayer alone, however sincere, could not undo? How did you respond?
Jeremiah says God's words became his joy and his heart's delight even in the middle of suffering. What would it look like for you to find that same joy in Scripture this week?
God promises to make Jeremiah a fortified wall of bronze against his opponents. Where do you need to trust God's promised strength rather than your own ability to withstand pressure?

translationNotes.note: Drought appears among the covenant curses named in Deuteronomy 28:23-24, tying this natural disaster directly to the broken covenant theme running through these chapters.

Moses and Samuel were remembered as Israel's two greatest historical intercessors, Exodus 32:11-14 and 1 Samuel 7:8-9 and 12:19-23, making the statement that even they could not prevail here especially severe.
I bear Your name may allude to being formally called by God's own name, marking Jeremiah as one who belongs to the LORD in a way that carries responsibility as well as identity.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit and sermon link verified via live web fetch this session confirming Don Carson's sermon covering Jeremiah 11 to 15 at thegospelcoalition.org.
